statement.markdown: Given the head of a doubly linked list, reverse the list and return the head of the reversed list. The doubly linked list node has two pointers, `next` and `prev`, pointing to the next and previous nodes in the sequence of nodes. The given list is 1-indexed. You are required to reverse the list in-place, meaning you must not allocate any new nodes, only re-arrange the existing nodes. The function should take the head of the list as input and return the head of the reversed list.
statement.inputFormat: The first line contains a single integer n, the number of nodes in the list. The next line contains n space-separated integers representing the values of the nodes in the list. Each node value is between 1 and 10000.
statement.outputFormat: The function should return the head of the reversed list, with the node values separated by spaces.
statement.notes: You can assume that the input list is non-empty and that the nodes are 1-indexed.

constraints.description: The number of nodes in the list
The values of the nodes in the list

examples.explanation: The input list is [1, 2, 3, 4]. After reversing the list, the output is [4, 3, 2, 1].
The input list is [5, 6]. After reversing the list, the output is [6, 5].
The input list is [10]. After reversing the list, the output is [10].
The input list is [7, 8, 9]. After reversing the list, the output is [9, 8, 7].

hints.text: to reverse a doubly linked list in-place, you need to traverse the list and update the next and prev pointers of each node. start by defining a function that takes the head of the list as input and returns the head of the reversed list.
you can use a temporary pointer to keep track of the previous node as you traverse the list. this will help you update the prev pointers of each node correctly.
when you encounter a node, you need to update its next and prev pointers to point to the previous node and the next node respectively. this will effectively reverse the link between the two nodes.
since the list is 1-indexed, you need to handle the special case of the first node separately. you can use a separate pointer to keep track of the new head of the reversed list.

by updating the next and prev pointers of each node in-place, you can reverse the entire list without allocating any new nodes. this approach ensures that the function runs in O(n) time and uses O(1) space.

editorial.title: Reversing a Doubly Linked List
editorial.intuition: The key observation is that reversing a doubly linked list can be achieved by updating the next and previous pointers of each node in a single pass, which significantly reduces the time complexity compared to a naive approach.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve traversing the list to store its nodes in an array, then iterating over the array in reverse order to construct a new reversed list. This approach is correct but slow, with a time complexity of O(n) but also requires extra space to store the nodes, resulting in a space complexity of O(n).
editorial.betterSolution: The optimal approach involves updating the pointers in place.
editorial.optimalSolution: Initialize three pointers: previous, current, and next. Traverse the list, for each node, do: current.next = previous; current.prev = next; then move previous and current one step forward. At the end of the traversal, reverse the head pointer.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The proof of correctness lies in the fact that we are iterating through the entire list once, updating the next and previous pointers correctly for each node. This ensures that after the iteration, the 'next' and 'prev' pointers of all nodes are correctly set for the reversed list, and the head pointer points to the new first node (which was the last node in the original list).
editorial.dryRun: For a list 1 <-> 2 <-> 3, initially, prev = None, current = 1, next = 2. First iteration: 1.next = None, 1.prev = 2; prev = 1, current = 2, next = 3. Second iteration: 2.next = 1, 2.prev = 3; prev = 2, current = 3, next = None. Third iteration: 3.next = 2, 3.prev = None; prev = 3. So, the new head is 3.
commonMistakes: Not updating the head pointer at the end.
Incorrectly updating the next and previous pointers.
Assuming extra space is needed for the optimal solution.
